nativeRange.habitat: Clear water over rocky reefs at depths of roughly 1–40 m; reportedly common off Ghana in rocky areas

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding on sponges, tunicates, jellyfish, soft corals, algae, and plankton. In captivity, generally advised to offer a varied diet including marine algae and Spirulina sheets, sponge-containing angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp, and other meaty items. Feeding two to three times daily tends to be recommended, as for most large angelfishes.

summary: A rare large angelfish from the tropical eastern Atlantic with a striking blue, orange and yellow adult coloration. Generally considered intermediate in difficulty and best kept singly in a large reef-adjacent aquarium.
careNotes: The West African Angelfish is one of the less commonly traded large angelfishes; it has historically been considered rare in the hobby but has become somewhat more available since the late 2010s. A spacious aquarium with abundant live rock for grazing and several open swimming lanes is generally advised. Both retail sources surveyed list a 150 US gal (≈568 L) minimum; specialists tend to recommend more for a 45 cm-class Pomacanthid, so the minimum here is biased upward to around 180 US gal (≈700 L). Juveniles and adults are visually very distinct: juveniles are a deep blue with a yellow tail, and as they grow (typically beginning around 4–5 cm body length) an orange edge develops on the body and the blue gradually shifts toward yellow or olive-green. Generally considered reef-safe only with caution — individuals have been known to nip stony and soft corals and the mantles of tridacnid clams. Best introduced as one of the last fish to an established system, and typically kept singly: keeping two adults together is normally discouraged unless the tank is exceptionally large. As with most large Pomacanthus-class angelfishes, this species is commonly considered prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when water quality is poor or the diet lacks vitamin-rich vegetable matter. Sponge-containing prepared foods tend to be recommended. Cross-source agreement on the high-stakes fields was good: LiveAquaria and BlueZoo Aquatics agree on tank (150 gal), reef-safety (with caution), temperament (semi-aggressive), care level (moderate/intermediate), and diet (omnivore); FishBase and Wikipedia agree on adult max size (≈45 cm) and confirm the Eastern Atlantic range from Senegal to the Congo River estuary.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reliably documented. As for related Holacanthus species, wild spawning is presumed to be pelagic with broadcast egg release. No established captive-breeding protocol is known.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source — provided temperature, pH, salinity, tank size, care level, temperament, reef-safety, diet and the juvenile-vs-adult color phase description.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (agrees at 150 gal), care level (intermediate), temperament (semi-aggressive), reef-compatibility (with caution), diet (omnivore), and provided the 'Guinean Angelfish' / 'Africanus Angelfish' synonyms.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult max size (45 cm TL confirmed), depth range (1–40 m), distribution (Senegal to DRC), and taxonomy (Pomacanthidae / Acanthuriformes).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirmed native range detail (Senegal to Congo River estuary including Cape Verde and São Tomé), diet (sponges, tunicates, jellyfish, soft corals, algae, plankton), and IUCN Least Concern status.

fish.breedingNotes: No established captive-breeding protocol is known. Wild spawning is presumed pelagic with broadcast egg release, as in related Holacanthus.
